title: Git Mastery Bible
subtitle: Repositories, staging, history shaping, collaboration, and safe recovery workflows

sections.intro: Learn the model behind every Git command: objects, refs, and snapshots.

blocks.title: Git stores objects and pointers
blocks.html:
Git is a content-addressed store of blobs, trees, commits, and refs.

That model explains why branches are cheap and history rewriting is possible.

  • Blobs store file content.
  • Trees store directory layout.
  • Commits point to trees and parents.
  • Refs move across commits.

items.label: Repository
items.html: Object database plus refs and config.

items.label: Working tree
items.html: Editable files on disk, not history.

items.label: HEAD
items.html: Pointer to a branch ref or directly to a commit.

blocks.title: How a commit becomes history
steps: Write a commit with parent links.

blocks.title: Inspect the object model
blocks.code:
git init demo
cd demo
echo hello > note.txt
git add note.txt
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git cat-file -p HEAD
blocks.caption: Git exposes its internals directly when you ask.

qa.question: What does Git store?
qa.answerHtml: Git stores blobs, trees, commits, tags, and refs.

qa.question: Why are branches lightweight?
qa.answerHtml: Because a branch is only a movable ref to a commit.

qa.question: What information changes a Git commit hash?
qa.answerHtml: A commit hash changes when its content, metadata, tree reference, or parent relationship changes, which is why rebases and amended commits create new ids.

sections.intro: Separate working tree, staging area, and committed history.

blocks.title: Three everyday Git states
blocks.html:
The working tree, index, and HEAD snapshot are different layers.

git add changes the index, and git commit records the index.

  • Working tree is editable.
  • Index is the next commit snapshot.
  • HEAD is already committed history.

blocks.title: Working tree vs index vs HEAD

items.label: Working tree
items.html: Safe place for edits and experiments.

items.label: Index
items.html: What the next commit will contain.

items.label: HEAD
items.html: What the branch already points to.

blocks.title: What add and commit do
steps: git add copies content into the index.
git commit writes the index as a new commit.

blocks.title: Trap: mixed commits
blocks.html: If you never inspect the staged diff, unrelated edits and debug code are easy to commit by accident.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: git commit records the staged snapshot.
Patch staging is a core skill.
Read the staged diff before commit.

qa.question: What is the staging area?
qa.answerHtml: It is the snapshot Git will use for the next commit.

qa.question: Why can one file be both staged and unstaged?
qa.answerHtml: Because the index can differ from HEAD while the working tree differs from the index.

qa.question: When is git add -p especially valuable on a fast-moving team?
qa.answerHtml: It is valuable when one file mixes refactors, fixes, and experiments, because it lets you build a clean reviewable commit instead of shipping noisy history.

sections.intro: Branching becomes powerful when you understand merge, rebase, and fast-forward precisely.

blocks.title: History shape is a workflow decision
blocks.html:
Branches are cheap, but history choices are not.

Merge preserves topology while rebase rewrites ancestry for a cleaner story.

  • Merge keeps the graph.
  • Rebase rewrites commit ancestry.
  • Interactive rebase reshapes local history.

items.label: Merge
items.html: Joins histories without rewriting existing commits.

items.label: Rebase
items.html: Replays commits on a new base as new commit ids.

items.label: Fast-forward
items.html: Moves a ref directly when there is no divergence.

blocks.title: What rebase does
steps: Find commits unique to the branch.
Replay them on the new base.
Move the branch to the new chain.

blocks.title: Drill: merge or rebase?
blocks.prompt: A feature branch is behind main and has never been pushed. What is usually cleaner?
blocks.answerHtml: Rebasing is usually cleaner because no one else depends on the old commit ids.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Shared branches need caution with rebase.

qa.question: What is the difference between merge and rebase?
qa.answerHtml: Merge joins histories, while rebase rewrites commits onto a new base.

qa.question: Why do rebased commits get new ids?
qa.answerHtml: Because parent relationships change, and parent metadata is part of the commit id.

qa.question: Why does rebasing a shared branch create team pain even if the code stays correct?
qa.answerHtml: Because the commit ids change, so teammates now have different history and must reconcile their local work against the rewritten branch.

sections.intro: Remote work becomes safer when fetch, pull, and push are explicit in your head.

blocks.title: Remote commands update refs and move data
blocks.html:
A remote is another repository Git knows how to talk to.

fetch updates remote-tracking refs, pull also integrates, and push requests remote ref updates.

  • origin/main is a remote-tracking ref.
  • fetch is non-destructive to your current branch.
  • push can be rejected for safety or policy reasons.

items.label: Fetch
items.html: Downloads objects and updates remote-tracking refs only.

items.label: Pull
items.html: Fetches and then merges or rebases.

items.label: Push
items.html: Asks the remote to accept new ref positions.

blocks.title: A safe collaboration flow
steps: Push only after review and checks.

blocks.title: Inspect before you integrate
blocks.code:
git fetch origin
git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main
git rebase origin/main
git push origin feature/api-cleanup
blocks.caption: This keeps the integration step visible instead of hidden.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: push updates remote refs if accepted.
force-with-lease is safer than plain force.

qa.question: What is the difference between fetch and pull?
qa.answerHtml: fetch updates remote-tracking refs only, while pull also integrates the changes.

qa.question: Why is force-with-lease safer than force?
qa.answerHtml: It refuses to overwrite remote history you have not seen.

qa.question: What does pull.rebase change about daily collaboration?
qa.answerHtml: It changes pull from fetch-plus-merge to fetch-plus-rebase, which can keep local history linear but also rewrites local commits more often.

sections.intro: Conflicts are intent decisions, not just text-marker cleanup.

blocks.title: Resolve conflicts by preserving behavior
blocks.html:
Git needs help when overlapping changes cannot be combined automatically.

The real task is choosing the right final behavior, not only deleting markers.

items.label: Merge conflict
items.html: Appears while combining histories.

items.label: Rebase conflict
items.html: Appears while replaying one commit at a time.

items.label: Binary conflict
items.html: Needs a manual decision because Git cannot merge it textually.

blocks.title: Trap: marker cleanup without understanding
blocks.html: A file can be conflict-free and still wrong if you solved the markers but not the intent.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Rebase conflicts narrow focus to one replayed commit.
Review the final file as a whole.

qa.question: What causes a Git conflict?
qa.answerHtml: A conflict happens when Git cannot reconcile overlapping changes automatically.

qa.question: Why can rebase conflicts be easier to reason about?
qa.answerHtml: Because they are shown one replayed commit at a time.

qa.question: What should you verify after resolving a conflict besides removing the markers?
qa.answerHtml: You should verify that the resulting code preserves the intended behavior, still builds, and still passes the relevant tests or checks.

sections.intro: Tags and releases turn history into stable milestones.

blocks.title: Tags mark immutable milestones
blocks.html:
A tag points to a specific object, usually a release commit.

Annotated tags are usually better release markers than lightweight tags.

items.label: Lightweight
items.html: Simple ref to a commit.

items.label: Annotated
items.html: Tag object with metadata and optional signature.

items.label: Release branch
items.html: Mutable line of work, not the same as a release marker.

blocks.title: Release tagging flow
steps: Use it for notes and deployment provenance.

blocks.title: Create a release tag
blocks.code:
git tag -a v1.4.0 -m "Release v1.4.0"
git push origin v1.4.0
blocks.caption: Annotated tags are the safer default for releases.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Annotated tags are better for releases.
Branches and tags solve different problems.

qa.question: What is a Git tag?
qa.answerHtml: A named reference to a specific object, usually a commit.

qa.question: Why is a release tag more trustworthy than a branch name?
qa.answerHtml: Because a branch can move, while a tag should stay on one immutable snapshot.

qa.question: Why might a release pipeline prefer annotated tags over lightweight tags?
qa.answerHtml: Annotated tags preserve metadata, support signing, and behave more like durable release records than a plain movable-looking ref.

sections.intro: Inspection tools let you answer history questions with evidence.

blocks.title: Use the right lens for the question
blocks.html:
git log, show, diff, blame, and pickaxe searches answer different change questions.

Strong Git users choose the inspection tool that matches the problem instead of guessing.

items.label: git log
items.html: Best for chronology and branch graphs.

items.label: show and diff
items.html: Best for patch content.

items.label: blame and pickaxe
items.html: Best for tracing lines or symbols backward.

blocks.title: Investigating a regression
steps: Use bisect if the range stays too large.

blocks.title: Drill: introduction commit
blocks.prompt: What search pattern is often useful for finding the commit that introduced a symbol?
blocks.answerHtml: Pickaxe search with git log -S is a strong starting point because it looks for commits where the string count changed.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Pickaxe and blame are tracing tools.

qa.question: What is git blame for?
qa.answerHtml: It shows which commit and author last changed each line.

qa.question: Why can blame be misleading?
qa.answerHtml: The last modifier may only have moved or reformatted the line, not introduced the behavior.

qa.question: When is git bisect a better choice than manual log inspection?
qa.answerHtml: It is better when the regression range is broad and reproducible, because bisect cuts the search space quickly by testing midpoint commits.

sections.intro: Recovery commands are powerful because they touch different layers of Git's model.

blocks.title: Recovery tools are not interchangeable
blocks.html:
revert, reset, restore, and reflog each solve a different problem.

Recovery gets easier when you ask whether you need to change published history, the index, or only the working tree.

items.label: revert
items.html: Safest for undoing already shared history.

items.label: reset
items.html: Moves refs and can also change index or working tree.

items.label: reflog
items.html: Helps recover previous ref positions after mistakes.

blocks.title: Recover after a mistaken reset
steps: Create a rescue branch or reset back to it.

blocks.title: Recover with reflog
blocks.code:
git reflog
git switch -c recover-work HEAD@{3}
blocks.caption: Creating a rescue branch first is a low-risk move.

qa.question: When should you use revert instead of reset?
qa.answerHtml: When the bad commit is already shared and you want to undo it without rewriting published history.

qa.question: Why is hard reset risky?
qa.answerHtml: Because it can discard work and move history at the same time.

qa.question: Why is revert usually safer than reset during a production incident on a shared branch?
qa.answerHtml: Because revert creates a new undo commit without rewriting published history that other teammates and automation may already depend on.

sections.intro: Team workflows are agreements about branch lifetime, review, release, and rollback.

blocks.title: Workflow quality is a delivery choice
blocks.html:
Feature-branch, trunk-based, and release-branch models reflect different tradeoffs around integration and delivery.

The best workflow is the one that reduces hidden integration cost for the team.

items.label: Feature branches
items.html: Good for isolation and review, but long-lived branches drift.

items.label: Trunk-based
items.html: Promotes frequent integration and fast release flow.

items.label: GitFlow-style
items.html: Can help some release-heavy teams, but often adds ceremony modern teams no longer need.

blocks.title: Trap: branch strategy without discipline
blocks.html: No branch model can compensate for weak tests, vague release ownership, or poor review standards.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Branch strategy is a delivery choice.

qa.question: What is a feature-branch workflow?
qa.answerHtml: Developers build work on separate branches and merge it back after review or validation.

qa.question: What makes trunk-based development succeed?
qa.answerHtml: Frequent integration, strong automation, protected branches, and safe rollout practices.

qa.question: What makes trunk-based development fail in practice?
qa.answerHtml: It fails when teams lack strong automation, small batch habits, and safe rollout controls, because frequent integration alone is not enough.

sections.intro: Large repositories and submodules introduce scale and coordination costs.

blocks.title: Scale changes the Git workflow
blocks.html:
Monorepos can simplify coordinated changes and shared tooling, but they increase performance and ownership demands.

Submodules preserve repository separation, but they add extra cognitive overhead because the parent repo only tracks a nested commit pointer.

items.label: Monorepo
items.html: Great for coordinated changes, but needs scale discipline.

items.label: Multi-repo
items.html: Simpler isolation, but harder cross-cutting change.

items.label: Submodules
items.html: Useful for exact pinning, but easy to misuse.

blocks.title: Large-repo hygiene
steps: Use sparse or partial workflows only where they fit.
Treat submodule updates as two-part changes.

blocks.title: Drill: submodule confusion
blocks.prompt: Why is a parent repo commit not enough after changing code inside a submodule?
blocks.answerHtml: Because the nested repository itself must have its own new commit recorded and pushed before the parent can safely point to it.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Monorepos need ownership and tooling discipline.
Submodules track commit pointers, not floating branches.
Large-repo choices are workflow choices too.

qa.question: What does a Git submodule record?
qa.answerHtml: A specific commit of another repository.

qa.question: Why can large repositories feel slow?
qa.answerHtml: Because object count, path count, history size, and working-tree scans all increase unless the repo is maintained carefully.

qa.question: Why do submodules surprise teams during deployment work?
qa.answerHtml: Because the parent repository tracks only a nested commit pointer, so deployment can break if the referenced submodule commit was never pushed or reviewed properly.

sections.intro: Hooks and automation turn Git events into quality feedback loops.

blocks.title: Automation should make the safe path easier
blocks.html:
Local hooks can catch cheap mistakes early, while CI and protected branches provide shared trusted enforcement.

The best automation is visible, predictable, and fast enough that people do not learn to bypass it.

items.label: Local hooks
items.html: Fast but easier to bypass or misconfigure.

items.label: CI
items.html: Trusted shared validation in a consistent environment.

items.label: Branch protection
items.html: Makes policy survive even when local automation is skipped.

blocks.title: Healthy automation layering
steps: Use local hooks for cheap checks.

blocks.title: Simple pre-commit idea
blocks.caption: Keep local checks short enough that developers do not resent them.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: CI is the shared truth source.
Automation should reduce risk without becoming opaque.

qa.question: What is a Git hook?
qa.answerHtml: A script Git runs automatically on certain repository events.

qa.question: Why should important rules live in CI too?
qa.answerHtml: Because local hooks can be bypassed or misconfigured, while CI runs consistently for everyone.

qa.question: What belongs in a pre-push hook instead of a pre-commit hook?
qa.answerHtml: Heavier checks such as broader test suites belong closer to pre-push or CI so local commit flow stays fast while higher-risk changes still get validated.

sections.intro: Security and signing strengthen trust in repository history and collaboration workflows.

blocks.title: Trust comes from process and cryptography
blocks.html:
Commit signing, protected branches, least privilege, and secret hygiene all contribute to Git trust.

A signed commit alone is not enough if the surrounding workflow is weak.

items.label: Signing
items.html: Verifies provenance from a trusted key.

items.label: Protected branches
items.html: Reduce accidental or malicious history damage.

items.label: Secret hygiene
items.html: Matters because history leaks are hard to clean fully.

blocks.title: Hardening a shared repository
steps: Limit who can rewrite sensitive refs.

blocks.title: Trap: rewriting history after a secret leak and stopping there
blocks.html: Even after rewriting history, the safer response is still credential rotation because the secret may already have been copied elsewhere.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Least privilege applies to Git workflows too.

qa.question: What is commit signing?
qa.answerHtml: A cryptographic signature proving the commit came from a trusted key.

qa.question: Why should a leaked secret still be rotated?
qa.answerHtml: Because rewriting history does not guarantee the secret was never copied or cached elsewhere.

qa.question: Why is signed provenance not enough without protected branches?
qa.answerHtml: Because a valid signature proves who authored a commit, but branch protection still controls whether unsafe or unreviewed history reaches critical refs.

sections.intro: Git performance and internals matter most when repositories get large and daily commands slow down.

blocks.title: Performance follows repository shape
blocks.html:
Packfiles, index size, path count, and filesystem behavior all influence Git speed.

You do not need plumbing mastery, but object and ref awareness helps you debug scale issues without guesswork.

items.label: Clone and fetch
items.html: Driven heavily by transfer and packfile behavior.

items.label: Status and checkout
items.html: Often dominated by local filesystem and index scans.

items.label: Garbage collection
items.html: Improves repository health when used thoughtfully.

blocks.title: Why repositories slow down
steps: More objects mean more storage and lookup work.
Bigger indexes cost more to scan.
Huge working trees increase filesystem traversal cost.

blocks.title: Drill: locate the bottleneck
blocks.prompt: Clone is fast from a nearby mirror, but git status is slow. Which layer should you suspect first?
blocks.answerHtml: The working-tree and index layer, not the network, because status is mostly local filesystem and index work.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Git speed depends on objects, packfiles, index size, and filesystem behavior.
Large-repo pain is often local too.
Internals knowledge helps target the right fix.

qa.question: What is a packfile?
qa.answerHtml: A packed storage format for many Git objects that improves transfer and storage efficiency.

qa.question: Why does internals knowledge help performance debugging?
qa.answerHtml: Because it helps you map a slow command to storage, refs, transfer, or filesystem causes instead of guessing.

qa.question: Why can repository size hurt developer experience even if CI stays fast?
qa.answerHtml: Because local commands such as status, checkout, and branch switching depend on index size, path count, and filesystem behavior on developer machines.

sections.intro: This capstone chapter pressure-tests command choice, recovery safety, and collaboration judgment.

blocks.title: Senior Git answers are layer-aware and safety-aware
blocks.html:
Good Git interview answers explain which layer a command affects, what risk it carries, and what the recovery path would be.

The strongest answers also state assumptions about branch sharing before naming a command.

items.label: Weak
items.html: Names a command without assumptions or rollback thinking.

items.label: Better
items.html: Explains what the command changes and when to use it.

items.label: Strong
items.html: Adds branch-sharing assumptions, blast radius, recovery, and verification thinking.

blocks.title: Structure of a strong Git answer
steps: Identify whether the issue is in the working tree, index, refs, or remote history.
Choose the least destructive tool for that layer.
Verify with status, log, or diff.

blocks.title: Capstone drill
blocks.prompt: You force-pushed a rebased shared branch and a teammate says their history exploded. What is your first move?
blocks.answerHtml: Pause more pushes, recover the previous tip from reflog or remote history, create rescue refs, and choose a recovery path the whole team can follow safely.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Good Git answers identify the changed layer.
Shared-history safety matters in every serious Git decision.
Recovery and verification are part of the answer.
Senior answers state assumptions before naming commands.

qa.question: What is the difference between git reset and git revert?
qa.answerHtml: reset moves refs and can rewrite local history, while revert adds a new undo commit.

qa.question: What does git cherry-pick do?
qa.answerHtml: It replays a selected commit onto the current branch as a new commit.

qa.question: Why is git fetch usually safer than git pull?
qa.answerHtml: Because fetch updates tracking refs without changing your current branch yet.

qa.question: What is reflog for?
qa.answerHtml: It helps recover previous ref positions after resets, rebases, and similar moves.

qa.question: How do you undo the last commit without losing the work?
qa.answerHtml: Use a soft or mixed reset depending on whether you want the changes staged or unstaged afterward.

qa.question: When would you use force-with-lease?
qa.answerHtml: When you intentionally rewrite a remote branch but want protection against overwriting newer remote changes you have not seen.

qa.question: Why can a merge commit be preferable on a team branch?
qa.answerHtml: Because it preserves shared history and avoids rewriting commits others may already depend on.

qa.question: How would you explain the index to an interviewer?
qa.answerHtml: It is Git's staging snapshot for the next commit, separate from the working tree and HEAD.

qa.question: What is detached HEAD?
qa.answerHtml: HEAD points directly to a commit instead of a branch ref.

qa.question: When should you rebase?
qa.answerHtml: Usually on private or narrowly shared branches when you want cleaner history or a new base.

qa.question: A teammate ran git reset --hard on the wrong branch locally. What is your first recovery thought?
qa.answerHtml: Check reflog quickly because the lost branch tip is often still reachable there.

qa.question: Why can a clean linear history still be the wrong choice?
qa.answerHtml: Because collaboration safety can matter more than perfect history aesthetics.

qa.question: What is the safest way to back out a bad commit on main that already deployed?
qa.answerHtml: Usually revert it with a new commit, verify, and redeploy.

qa.question: Why can git blame point to the wrong person?
qa.answerHtml: The last modifier may only have moved or reformatted the line rather than introduced the bug.

qa.question: How would you recover from a mistaken force push on a shared branch?
qa.answerHtml: Freeze more pushes, find the old tip, create rescue refs, communicate, and restore or realign safely.

qa.question: Why do commit ids change after rebase?
qa.answerHtml: Because commit ids include parent relationships, and rebasing changes parentage.

qa.question: How do you decide between cherry-pick and merge for a hotfix?
qa.answerHtml: Cherry-pick is for one isolated fix; merge is better when you want the full branch context.

qa.question: What would make you suspicious of a team's Git workflow?
qa.answerHtml: Frequent shared force pushes, weak protection, vague release provenance, and recovery that depends on one expert.

qa.question: Why is 'just use hard reset' often a weak answer?
qa.answerHtml: Because it ignores branch-sharing assumptions, data-loss risk, and published-history safety.

qa.question: What would you inspect before deleting a long-lived branch?
qa.answerHtml: Confirm it is merged or unused and that no deployment or automation still depends on it.

qa.question: How do you talk about Git maturity in system design?
qa.answerHtml: By connecting branching, review, release tags, rollback, automation, provenance, and recovery into one collaboration model.

qa.question: What makes a Git answer sound senior?
qa.answerHtml: It states assumptions, chooses the least risky command, explains tradeoffs, and names the recovery path.

qa.question: Why is force-with-lease safer than force?
qa.answerHtml: It protects you from overwriting remote history that moved since your last known state.

qa.question: Why should verification be part of every Git answer?
qa.answerHtml: Because the command is not done until you know the repository now says what you intended.

qa.question: How would you explain the difference between restore and checkout in modern Git?
qa.answerHtml: restore focuses on file and index state recovery, while checkout historically combined branch switching and file restoration in ways that were easier to misuse.

qa.question: Why is fetch-plus-inspect usually a healthier habit than blind pull?
qa.answerHtml: Because it keeps remote change discovery separate from integration, which reduces surprise and makes history decisions more deliberate.

qa.question: What makes a commit history good for future debugging, not just code review?
qa.answerHtml: Focused commits, meaningful messages, and logical grouping make it much easier to trace regressions and understand why behavior changed.

qa.question: How do you talk about squash merges versus preserving full branch history?
qa.answerHtml: Squash merges keep the target branch compact, while preserving full history retains granular commit context. The right choice depends on how much branch detail the team wants to keep.

qa.question: When is cherry-pick the wrong answer for a hotfix?
qa.answerHtml: It is the wrong answer when the fix depends on broader branch context that would be unsafe or misleading to separate.

qa.question: Why does force-with-lease still require team communication?
qa.answerHtml: Because even a safer force push can still invalidate teammates' local history and workflows if they do not know it happened.

qa.question: What is the value of signed tags beyond signed commits?
qa.answerHtml: Signed tags strengthen release provenance because release automation and audit trails often center on tags rather than individual commits.

qa.question: How would you recover a branch after an accidental delete?
qa.answerHtml: Use reflog, remote hosting history, or other surviving refs to find the old tip, then recreate the branch from that commit.

qa.question: Why can a repository with tidy branches still have a weak Git workflow?
qa.answerHtml: Because branch names do not guarantee good automation, review discipline, rollback clarity, or safe collaboration habits.

qa.question: What is a strong answer to 'How do you avoid Git disasters on a team?'
qa.answerHtml: Use protected branches, clear release tagging, small focused branches, explicit recovery expectations, and automation that catches risky changes early.

qa.question: How should you reason about deleting local changes during cleanup?
qa.answerHtml: First decide whether the target is the working tree, the index, or local history, then choose the least destructive command that solves exactly that layer.

qa.question: What is the senior mistake in saying 'Git is just version control'?
qa.answerHtml: It ignores that Git is also a collaboration protocol, a release-provenance tool, and a recovery system whose workflow design shapes team reliability.
